preFix.title: Annual maintenance for a composting toilet
preFix.subtitle: Emptying, cleaning, and servicing a self-contained composting toilet unit
preFix.excerpt: A self-contained composting toilet requires emptying the compost chamber once or twice a year, cleaning the bowl and seals, and checking the ventilation fan. Done on schedule, the job takes two hours and produces a bucket of finished compost that can go on ornamental borders or be buried in the garden. Neglected, the chamber fills, venting fails, and the unit smells. This tutorial covers the annual service for a standard residential composting toilet.
preFix.sourceNotes: Sun-Mar composting toilet installation and maintenance manual 2024. Separett Villa service guide. Envirolet composting toilet technical support documentation.

content.text: breaks down 80 to 90% of waste volume through aerobic decomposition. With one person using it, the chamber needs emptying once a year. With four people, twice a year. The removed material has typically been composting for 6 to 12 months. It is pathogen-reduced humus suitable for use on non-edible crops.

Composting toilet output from a self-contained domestic unit is not sterilised compost. It has reduced pathogen levels compared with raw waste. But it should not be applied to edible crops, root vegetables. Or to any surface where food preparation takes place. Bury it at least 150 mm deep in an ornamental border. Or apply to the base of fruit trees and shrubs where the material will not contact edible parts directly.
Turn the drum or rotate the chamber following the manufacturer's instructions. This moves the oldest compost to the collection tray or releases the mature layer.

Remove the compost collection tray or open the lower access panel. Transfer the material into a bucket using a small hand trowel or scoop. Wear nitrile gloves and a dust mask. The material should look like dark soil, not recognisably like its input. If it is still clearly undecomposed. Close the chamber and allow a further 3 to 6 months before emptying.
Add a bulking agent. After emptying, put a small amount of peat or coir or a recommended compost additive into the chamber. This restarts microbial activity.

Inspect the ventilation pipe and fan. The fan should run continuously when the unit is in use. A fan failure is the most common cause of odour from a composting toilet. Test by feeling for airflow at the top of the ventilation stack; if there is none. Check the power supply and replace the fan.

Clean the bowl and seat with a mild non-antibacterial cleaner. Antibacterial cleaners can kill the microorganisms in the composting chamber if used in quantity. Use the manufacturer's recommended cleaner or a dilute castile soap solution.

Inspect the liquid drain. Most self-contained composting toilets have a liquid diversion system or a liquid overflow drain. Check that the drain is flowing freely and has not blocked with scale or deposits.

attrs.fix: Check fan power and operation; clear any blockage in the vent pipe; confirm the vent exits above the roofline rather than into a confined space
attrs.cause: Ventilation fan has failed or ventilation pipe is blocked
attrs.symptom: Unit smells when the toilet is used

attrs.fix: Add dry bulking agent (coir, straw, sawdust from untreated wood) and improve liquid diversion; check urine diverter or liquid overflow drain
attrs.cause: Liquid separator is not functioning, too much liquid is entering the chamber, or not enough bulking agent has been added
attrs.symptom: Material in the chamber is wet and slimy rather than composting

initialFindings.message: unflagged-jargon: "nitrile" — use "protective gloves" unless the specific material is critical to the outcome
paragraph reads at grade 11.2 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
initialFindings.snippet: Clean the bowl and seat with a mild non-antibacterial cleaner. Antibacterial cleaners can kill the microorganisms in the composting chamber
